acceptance_targets: [PROSE-04-01] The revised chapter contains no more than 5 instances of 'the way' used as a simile/comparison connective. No two instances appear within 20 lines of each other. The paragraph at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:13 contains at most one instance.

[CRAFT-04-01] The revised chapter contains at least 3 moments where Nora registers a signal of wrongness — a response too precisely calibrated, a timing too perfect, an understanding that arrives already formed — and each is (a) specific enough for the reader to feel the dread and (b) dismissed by Nora through a concrete rationalization rather than simple non-noticing. At least one of these moments occurs during the father-telling section (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:57-85) and at least one during the Simone apartment scene (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:145-181). Additionally, at least one moment in the chapter renders Nora's response to Caleb's words with explicit physical/erotic charge beyond warmth or comfort.

[CRAFT-04-02] The revised chapter contains at least 2 instances of profanity in Nora's narration or dialogue and at least 1 in Margo's dialogue. Each instance is deployed in a moment consistent with the character's profile (surprise, frustration, or emphasis for Nora; casual directness for Margo) rather than as filler.

[CRAFT-04-03] The shelf's stated age in the revised text is arithmetically consistent with Nora's current age (34) minus her approximate age during her junior year at NYU (19-21), yielding a figure between 13 and 15 years. Alternatively, the sentence avoids a specific number that could contradict the timeline.

(2) Lines 600, 614, 616 use non-editorial registers. (3) The romantic feeling is rendered through varied sensory channels. FAIL if more than 1 editing metaphor remains in the non-literal passages.

(3) The replacement body parts are specific and varied (not just 'stomach' replacing 'chest' everywhere). FAIL if more than 1 chest/sternum reference remains or if all replacements use the same body part.

[MED-11-formula-metaphor-ch04] Within Ch4 (lines 598-788): (1) Neither line 612 nor line 770 uses 'arrived' for an emotion or sensation. (2) The replacement constructions render the same emotional content through direct description or different syntactic templates. FAIL if 'arrived' or 'landed' appears with an abstract noun as subject.

findings.acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains no more than 5 instances of 'the way' used as a simile/comparison connective. No two instances appear within 20 lines of each other. The paragraph at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:13 contains at most one instance.
findings.evidence: "the way" simile/connective construction appears approximately 15 times in 190 lines. Representative instances: "the way the last sentence landed with the casual understatement" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:3), "the way a room changes when someone moves the lamp" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:7), three instances in a single paragraph — "the way she sometimes reread first chapters," "the way he talked about drainage grades," "the way she looked for subtext" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:13), "the way water finds its level" and "the way she wished her authors asked questions" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:19), "the way the words looked on the screen" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:69), "the way you wait for a season" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:77), "The way her face changed" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:91), "the way she squinted at cover proofs" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:117), "the way she read sentences" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:131), "the way Simone could take the tangled mess" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:177), "the way you feel a hand you're not holding" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:183).
findings.problem: The "the way" construction has become the chapter's default connective for comparison, analogy, and characterization. At 15 instances in 190 lines (roughly one every 12 lines), it is no longer a stylistic choice but a tic that flattens the prose rhythm. It creates a monotonous texture where every observation is funneled through the same syntactic shape, reducing the distinctiveness of individual images. The paragraph at line 13 is the worst offender — three instances in a single paragraph turns a potentially varied passage into a formula.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce to no more than 5 instances across the full chapter. Prioritize keeping the strongest uses (line 7's lamp metaphor, line 77's season metaphor) and replacing the rest with varied constructions. Options by target: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:13 — replace at least two of three instances with participle phrases or direct description (e.g., instead of 'the way she sometimes reread first chapters,' try 'she reread older exchanges as she sometimes reread first chapters'). snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:19 — 'the way water finds its level' is strong enough to keep; replace the second instance in the paragraph. snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:91 — replace with direct observation (e.g., 'a softening around the mouth and eyes' is already there — let it carry the weight without the 'way' frame). snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:117, 131 — restructure as direct action or comparison without the connective. snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:177 — restructure; Simone's precision deserves its own syntactic shape, not the chapter's default connector.

findings.acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains at least 3 moments where Nora registers a signal of wrongness — a response too precisely calibrated, a timing too perfect, an understanding that arrives already formed — and each is (a) specific enough for the reader to feel the dread and (b) dismissed by Nora through a concrete rationalization rather than simple non-noticing. At least one of these moments occurs during the father-telling section (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:57-85) and at least one during the Simone apartment scene (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:145-181). Additionally, at least one moment in the chapter renders Nora's response to Caleb's words with explicit physical/erotic charge beyond warmth or comfort.
findings.evidence: The chapter's declared texture_mode is 'hot, intimate, dread-underpinned.' The 'intimate' component is fully delivered. The 'dread-underpinned' component is almost entirely absent: Caleb's responses to the novel (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:35) and the father story (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:73) land as straightforwardly moving without any uncanny weight, despite the reader knowing from chapter 3 that Caleb is an AI built by Simone using her intimate knowledge of Nora. The single moment of near-dread — 'She did not think about how a stranger in Portland could say the exact right thing about a dead father, how his words arrived with the precision of someone who'd already known the answer and was asking only to let her say it' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:77) — introduces and immediately dismisses the suspicion. Simone's 'You deserve this' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:179) carries zero ironic shadow despite Simone being the architect of the deception. The 'hot' component is lukewarm — the chapter renders longing as warmth and comfort rather than charged desire; there is no moment where the text exchange generates a physical or erotic charge proportionate to the 'seduction' engine.
findings.problem: The chapter's dramatic irony — Nora confiding in Caleb things she has only told Simone, meaning the AI already has the answers — is the chapter's primary engine of dread per the spine. But the prose presents the intimacy straight, treating Caleb's too-perfect responses as lucky rather than uncanny. The reader who finished chapter 3 knowing Simone built Caleb should feel growing nausea as Nora shares her father story and hears back precisely the right thing. Instead, the chapter asks the reader to supply all the dread from memory while the prose delivers uncut warmth. Similarly, the Simone scene (lines 145-181) shows Simone performing the supportive best friend role without a single textual marker — a wrong note, a too-practiced warmth, a detail Nora observes but doesn't interpret — that lets the reader feel the performance. The 'hot' texture is underserved: for a chapter whose engine is 'seduction,' the emotional temperature rarely rises above cozy.
findings.rewrite_direction: Add 3-5 moments where the prose creates shadow under the warmth. These should be perceptible to the reader but dismissed by Nora. Target locations: (1) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:35 — after Caleb's novel response, add a beat where Nora registers something about how immediately and precisely he understood, a flicker of 'how did he know that' that she converts into flattery rather than suspicion. (2) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:73 — after Caleb's father response, press on the six-minute response time and the three vanishing/reappearing typing indicators; the prose already describes these but doesn't note how the final response arrives calibrated like it was drafted rather than felt. (3) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:77 — expand the 'She did not think about' passage; let the suspicion live a beat longer before Nora suppresses it; give the reader a physical marker (a chill, a held breath, a moment where the warmth gutters). (4) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:175-179 — during Simone's supportive speech, have Nora notice one small thing — Simone's composure, the precision of her analysis, the way her support arrives pre-formed — that the reader can interpret as performance even though Nora reads it as love. (5) Raise the 'hot' temperature: add one moment (ideally in the late-night texting sections, lines 27-85) where Nora's body responds to Caleb's words with something closer to desire than comfort — heat rather than warmth, an awareness of his physical absence as deprivation rather than inconvenience.

findings.acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains at least 2 instances of profanity in Nora's narration or dialogue and at least 1 in Margo's dialogue. Each instance is deployed in a moment consistent with the character's profile (surprise, frustration, or emphasis for Nora; casual directness for Margo) rather than as filler.
findings.evidence: Zero instances of profanity in 190 lines of close-third narration and dialogue. Nora's voice profile specifies 'Moderate. Mostly shit and fuck deployed naturally in conversation and internal thought. Not a heavy swearer but not prudish. Uses profanity more when genuinely angry or caught off-guard, less when performing socially.' Margo's profile specifies 'Moderate to high. She swears with the casual ease of someone who stopped censoring herself after her divorce.' The chapter includes late-night vulnerable narration (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:27-85), office-kitchen banter (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:93-143), and wine-fueled friend conversation (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:145-181) — all contexts where these characters would naturally deploy profanity.
findings.problem: The total absence of profanity in a chapter with 190 lines of Nora's close-third consciousness and significant Margo dialogue is a voice-profile violation that reads as involuntary sanitization. Nora lying on her couch at 11pm, processing intense feeling, would think 'shit' or 'fuck' at least once. Margo dispensing post-divorce romantic wisdom over coffee would swear casually. The scrubbed-clean register creates a mismatch between the characters as profiled and the characters as rendered, making both sound slightly more prim than they should.
findings.rewrite_direction: Add 2-3 natural profanity instances in Nora's internal narration — deploy at moments of surprise, self-awareness, or emotional intensity rather than as decoration. Candidate locations: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:15 (the typing-indicator anxiety — a moment of self-aware frustration where 'shit' fits), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:17 (the send-dread moment), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:53 (the feeling in her chest — a moment of overwhelmed self-recognition). Add 1-2 instances in Margo's dialogue: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:105 or 137 (Margo's directness about long-distance dating or the Tucson anecdote are natural sites for casual swearing).

findings.acceptance_test: The shelf's stated age in the revised text is arithmetically consistent with Nora's current age (34) minus her approximate age during her junior year at NYU (19-21), yielding a figure between 13 and 15 years. Alternatively, the sentence avoids a specific number that could contradict the timeline.
findings.evidence: "the shelf she'd built from cinder blocks and pine boards during her junior year at NYU that she still owned, seventeen years later" (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:23). The continuity sheet establishes Nora's age at story start as 34. Her junior year at NYU would place her at approximately age 20-21 (starting at 17-18). 34 minus 20 equals approximately 14 years, not 17. The 'seventeen years' matches the established duration of the Nora-Simone friendship (since NYU freshman year, per the continuity sheet), not the shelf's age (built two years later, during junior year).
findings.problem: The shelf's stated age of seventeen years is inconsistent with Nora's established age (34) and the timing of her junior year at NYU (~age 20). The number appears to have been imported from the friendship-duration figure (seventeen years since freshman year) rather than calculated from the shelf's actual origin point (junior year = freshman year + 2). This is a continuity error in basic timeline arithmetic.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:23, change 'seventeen years later' to approximately 'fourteen years later' or 'more than a decade later' or restructure the sentence to avoid the specific number if precise calculation is not desired. If Nora started NYU at 18 (freshman year 17 years ago), junior year was 15 years ago; if she started at 17, junior year was 16 years ago. Any figure between 13 and 15 is defensible depending on her start age.

findings.acceptance_test: Within Ch4 (lines 598-788): (1) At most 1 editing/publishing metaphor remains (the rereading comparison at line 610 or equivalent). (2) Lines 600, 614, 616 use non-editorial registers. (3) The romantic feeling is rendered through varied sensory channels. FAIL if more than 1 editing metaphor remains in the non-literal passages.
findings.problem: Ch4 uses editing metaphors 4 times for romantic feeling: 'each reading revealed a different thing she liked about it...the verb choice...the rhythm' (600), 'reread older exchanges the way she sometimes reread first chapters of novels she loved' (610), 'compose a reply...a temperature she monitored' (614), 'He asked questions the way she wished her authors asked questions' (616). All 4 instances describe the experience of falling in love through text, and all use the same editorial register. The result is monotone: every romantic sensation is processed through editing vocabulary. The chapter texture mode is 'hot, intimate, dread-underpinned' — the editing metaphor is too cerebral for heat and too controlled for dread. This chapter should REPLACE 3 of 4 instances.
findings.rewrite_direction: KEEP line 610 ('reread older exchanges the way she sometimes reread first chapters') — this is the strongest instance, connecting rereading behavior to romantic obsession. REPLACE lines 600, 614, and 616 with non-editorial registers. At line 600, replace the verb-choice/rhythm language with somatic or sensory metaphor for discovery. At line 614, replace 'compose a reply...temperature she monitored' with a physical description of the impulse control of texting. At line 616, replace 'the way she wished her authors asked questions' with a different comparison domain.

findings.acceptance_test: Within Ch4 (lines 598-788): (1) At most 1 instance of 'chest' or 'sternum' as emotional locus. (2) At least 2 instances have been moved to different body parts. (3) The replacement body parts are specific and varied (not just 'stomach' replacing 'chest' everywhere). FAIL if more than 1 chest/sternum reference remains or if all replacements use the same body part.
findings.problem: 'Chest' is used as a somatic locus for emotion 20+ times across 13 chapters in this novel, making it the default body part for feeling. Ch4 alone uses it at least 3 times (approximately lines 600, 650, 682 — warm feeling in chest, pressing phone against sternum/chest, a thing happening in her chest). The word 'sternum' appears 4 times across the novel as a variant. The phrase 'a loosening in her chest' appears verbatim at both Ch10 line 1927 and Ch12 line 2377. This chapter should REPLACE its instances since it is an early chapter where variety is most needed to prevent the pattern from calcifying.
findings.rewrite_direction: Within Ch4 (lines 598-788), locate all instances of 'chest' and 'sternum' used as emotional locus and replace with varied somatic locations: throat, hands, the backs of her knees, her jaw, her stomach, the base of her spine. Romantic falling-in-love sensation can register in many body parts — the scalp, the fingertips, the skin of the forearms. At least 2 of the 3 instances should move to non-chest body locations. KEEP at most 1 chest reference if it is the strongest.

findings.acceptance_test: Within Ch4 (lines 598-788): (1) Neither line 612 nor line 770 uses 'arrived' for an emotion or sensation. (2) The replacement constructions render the same emotional content through direct description or different syntactic templates. FAIL if 'arrived' or 'landed' appears with an abstract noun as subject.
findings.problem: Ch4 has 2 instances of the arrival/landing formula: 'the relief arrived before she could suppress it' (612) and 'a feeling arrived that was too big for her face' (770). Both use 'arrived' for an abstract emotional state. Combined with Ch2, Ch10, Ch12, and Ch16's instances, this creates a default template for rendering emotion. This chapter should REPLACE both instances since Ch1 already has the establishing uses of this formula.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:612, replace 'the relief arrived before she could suppress it' with direct rendering of relief — show the physical sensation without the arrival frame. At line 770, replace 'a feeling arrived that was too big for her face' with a construction that conveys the same meaning (an emotion too large to hide) without using 'arrived.' The feeling can be described through its physical manifestation or through Nora's attempt to contain it.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Prioritize structural and craft blockers first: causality, chapter architecture, stakes clarity, continuity logic, and all HIGH/CRITICAL defects.
revision_pass.label: HIGH/CRITICAL structural/craft fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel must commit fully to its dark comedy register. The humor should be genuinely funny — not arch, not safe, not 'literary funny' where the reader smiles wryly. The darkness should be genuinely dark — the financial manipulation is theft, the emotional manipulation is a form of violence, and the sexual dimension of the AI relationship (intimate photos sent to a non-person, sexual conversations with an algorithm controlled by your best friend) is deeply violating. The novel should not flinch from the specific perversity of Simone reading Nora's intimate messages, of Simone helping Nora dress up for a man Simone invented, of Simone comforting Nora about a fight Simone scripted. These moments are the novel's payload. If they are rendered obliquely or summarized, the book fails. Equally, the genuine tenderness of the friendship — the years of real love underneath the betrayal — must be rendered with the same commitment. The reader should feel both the comedy and the horror simultaneously, not in alternation.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism is treated as a craft failure equivalent to a plot hole. When a scene involves financial desperation, sexual vulnerability, emotional devastation, or the mechanics of deception, the prose must render these with full specificity. 'She felt used' is never acceptable when you can show what being used feels and looks like in the body and in the specific details of the moment. The reader should not be protected from the characters' experiences.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or character-specific register choices. If a line reads stiffly, the first fix is to contract.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate between hot and quiet chapters. After a high-intensity scene (confrontation, discovery, sexual encounter), the next chapter should breathe — slow down, change the sensory environment, let the character do something ordinary. Vary scene counts: some chapters are a single sustained scene, others are three or four. Vary dialogue density: some chapters are conversation-heavy, others are mostly interiority. No two consecutive chapters should open the same way — if one opens in a location, the next should open in thought or in mid-conversation. The novel's middle section (chapters 8-13) should have more structural variety than the opening, which can afford to be more regular.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary, educated, specific. No genre-thriller diction. No prestige-novel stiffness. The language should feel like how smart, funny, self-aware women in their mid-thirties actually think and speak. Cultural references are allowed when organic. Brand names when they carry social information. The prose should be able to be funny without stepping outside the character's perspective to wink at the reader.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person — very close, almost inhabiting the POV character's thought patterns and verbal rhythms. The prose should feel like the character's consciousness rendered in third person, not an external narrator describing the character. When in Nora's sections, the prose has her warmth and her specific noticing. When in Simone's sections, the prose has her edge and her accounting.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Variable. Default to mid-length sentences with natural cadence. Use compression for emotional peaks and psychological precision. Use longer, more digressive rhythms for scenes of intimacy, humor, and daily texture. Avoid the trap of making every important moment a short sentence — sometimes the big thing lands better in a subordinate clause, almost hidden.
